title: Creator Diversity Matters More Than Creator Fit Under Andromeda
insight: Meta's Andromeda algorithm treats ads from the same creator as nearly identical, limiting reach to new audiences. Having 50 diverse creators outperforms 5 perfect-fit creators because diversity of faces and personas directly expands the audience segments Meta can deliver to. The bottleneck isn't finding the right creator — it's having enough different ones.
source_title: The Real Math of UGC: 1000 Creatives/Month at Scale | Price Power Podcast Ep. 10
actionable_steps: Prioritize expanding your creator roster over perfecting a small pool of creators
Aim for diversity across demographics, energy levels, and visual presentation styles
Track reach per creator to identify when Andromeda is capping delivery due to similarity

title: Minimum Viable UGC Test: 20-40 Creators, 4-5 Concepts, $20-50K First Month
insight: A proper initial UGC test on one platform in one country (e.g., US on Meta) requires 20-40 creators across 4-5 concepts, with $20-50K minimum spend in the first month. Below that threshold, there's insufficient data to determine if UGC can scale for your app. Many teams under-invest in the initial test and wrongly conclude UGC doesn't work.
source_title: The Real Math of UGC: 1000 Creatives/Month at Scale | Price Power Podcast Ep. 10
actionable_steps: Budget $20-50K for the first month of a UGC test on a single platform/country
Source 20-40 diverse creators before launching the test
Prepare 4-5 distinct creative concepts to test across the creator pool
Do not conclude UGC doesn't work if you tested with fewer creators or lower spend

title: ~5% Creative Success Rate; Top Spenders Need 1000+ Creatives/Month
insight: Expect roughly 5% of UGC creatives to succeed. Even at 5-6 figure monthly spend, you need 20-50 new creatives per month due to ad fatigue. At 7-figure monthly spend, top clients produce 1000+ creatives per month. Creative lifespan is typically 2-3 weeks, and trend-based creatives burn out even faster. Plan production capacity around these numbers.
source_title: The Real Math of UGC: 1000 Creatives/Month at Scale | Price Power Podcast Ep. 10
actionable_steps: Plan for a ~5% creative success rate when forecasting production needs
At $100K+/month spend, produce at least 20-50 new creatives monthly
Refresh creatives every 2-3 weeks before fatigue degrades performance
Scale production capacity proportionally as spend increases

title: Two-Campaign Structure: CPI Test Then BAU Scale
insight: Split campaigns into a test campaign (optimized for CPI as a quick proxy signal) and a scale/BAU campaign (optimized for ROAS or subscriptions where all the budget goes). CPI winners from the test campaign get promoted into the scale campaign. This solves the slow feedback loop problem for subscription apps, where trial-to-subscription data can take weeks to materialize.
source_title: The Real Math of UGC: 1000 Creatives/Month at Scale | Price Power Podcast Ep. 10
actionable_steps: Create a dedicated test campaign optimized for CPI to get fast signal on new creatives
Run a separate BAU/scale campaign optimized for ROAS or subscription events
Promote CPI winners from the test campaign into the scale campaign
Use CPI as a leading indicator while waiting for downstream subscription data

title: Two Approaches for Scaling Winning UGC Concepts via Replication
insight: Once you have a winning UGC concept, scale it two ways: (A) Give new creators the broad concept plus app-specific guidelines and let them interpret in their own style — same hook/narrative/CTA but different faces and energy. (B) Use AI to replicate the exact structure and script — because real creators forced to stick to rigid scripts sound unnatural. Some of the best performing campaigns are replications. Approach B works when the script matters; Approach A works when the persona matters.
source_title: The Real Math of UGC: 1000 Creatives/Month at Scale | Price Power Podcast Ep. 10
actionable_steps: For persona-driven winners: brief new creators on the concept and let them bring their own style
For script-driven winners: use AI replication rather than forcing creators to follow rigid scripts
Test both replication approaches to find which works for each winning concept
Build a replication pipeline so winning concepts can be scaled within days of identification

title: TikTok vs Meta Creative Differences: Length, Structure, and Format
insight: Despite 'TikTokification' trends, platforms still behave differently. TikTok winners: very short (10s), no app demo, no CTA, organic/raw feel, trending sound, strong relatable hook, optimized for watch rate. Carousels with personal stories are underused but top-performing on TikTok (especially dating apps). Meta winners: longer (15-30s), clearer structured narratives, product demos, and use cases showing value directly. Don't assume the same creative works everywhere.
source_title: The Real Math of UGC: 1000 Creatives/Month at Scale | Price Power Podcast Ep. 10
actionable_steps: For TikTok: keep ads under 10 seconds, use trending sounds, skip CTAs and app demos
For TikTok dating/lifestyle apps: test carousel formats with personal stories
For Meta: use 15-30s ads with structured narratives and product demos
Create platform-specific creative rather than repurposing the same asset across platforms

title: US English Creatives Work in Non-English Markets
insight: Counterintuitively, top-performing US English ads often work in non-English speaking markets like France and Germany. Not as well as in the US, but they deliver results. Recommendation: test English ads in new markets AND produce local-language versions with local creators or AI dubbing. Do both rather than waiting for localized creative before expanding.
source_title: The Real Math of UGC: 1000 Creatives/Month at Scale | Price Power Podcast Ep. 10
actionable_steps: Test your top US English creatives in non-English markets before localizing
Simultaneously produce local-language versions with native creators or AI dubbing
Don't delay market expansion while waiting for localized creative — run English ads first
Compare performance of English vs localized creative to decide investment level per market

title: Winners Keep One Consistent Story From Ad to Paywall
insight: Winning subscription apps maintain one consistent narrative end-to-end: ad → store page → onboarding → paywall. If the ad promises 'sleep better in 7 minutes,' that exact promise and hero feature should appear at every touchpoint. UGC tends to underperform for products that need longer technical demos (heavy utilities, some games) — those scale better on Google/AppLovin where format allows it.
source_title: The Real Math of UGC: 1000 Creatives/Month at Scale | Price Power Podcast Ep. 10
actionable_steps: Audit the promise in your top ads and verify it's echoed on store page, onboarding, and paywall
Align hero features shown in ads with the first thing users see post-install
For products requiring complex technical demos, consider Google/AppLovin over Meta UGC
Test narrative consistency by walking through the full ad-to-paywall flow as a new user

title: Brief Sweet Spot: Structured Enough to Guide, Loose Enough for Happy Accidents
insight: Too-loose briefs produce lazy, low-quality output. Too-strict briefs kill the creative accidents that become top performers. The best UGC briefs provide structure (hook direction, narrative arc, CTA) while leaving room for creator interpretation. One creator's mispronunciation of a brand name led to a 350M view ad — that kind of outcome is impossible with rigid scripting.
source_title: The Real Math of UGC: 1000 Creatives/Month at Scale | Price Power Podcast Ep. 10
actionable_steps: Structure briefs around hook direction, narrative arc, and CTA — not word-for-word scripts
Leave explicit room in briefs for creator interpretation and personality
Review creator submissions for unexpected elements before dismissing them as off-brief
Track which top performers came from on-brief vs off-brief creative to calibrate brief strictness

title: AI Creators Best for Hard-to-Source Personas, Not Full Replacement
insight: AI-generated UGC excels in specific cases: hard-to-source personas (high income, pregnant women), complex scenes (street interviews, cinematic shots), and scaling winning concepts across variations (e.g., one winning job-persona ad expanded to dozens of jobs). But AI can't replace the happy accidents from real creators. Best strategy: find winning concepts with humans then scale with AI, or test cheaply with AI then produce winners with real creators.
source_title: The Real Math of UGC: 1000 Creatives/Month at Scale | Price Power Podcast Ep. 10
actionable_steps: Use AI creators for hard-to-source personas (specific demographics, professional roles)
Use AI to scale winning concepts across variations (e.g., same script with different job personas)
Keep real creators for concept discovery where happy accidents drive breakthroughs
Test a hybrid workflow: discover winners with humans, scale with AI (or vice versa)
